findings.summary: Docstring bloat on trivial wrapper functions in logging_utils.py
evidence: log_substep has a 10-line docstring for a 2-line function that calls logger.info with an f-string
log_stage has a 12-line docstring (with example output) for a 4-line function
log_success has a 1-line docstring but still has redundant emoji-selection logic that restates the function name
findings.suggestion: Reduce docstrings on trivial formatting helpers to a single-line description; remove example-output blocks from functions whose behavior is fully described by their name and signature.

findings.summary: Three thin module-level pass-through functions in health_check.py wrap a singleton with no added policy
evidence: quick_health_check() calls _get_checker().is_healthy() — one statement, no logic
get_health_status() calls _get_checker().get_health_status() — one statement, no logic
get_health_metrics() calls _get_checker().get_metrics() — one statement, no logic
findings.suggestion: Expose a module-level singleton directly or collapse into a single get_health() entry point, eliminating the three one-liner wrappers.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report unpacks ReportExtras field-by-field into locals that are immediately re-used only once
evidence: Lines 68-73 unpack all six fields of extras into identically-named locals
Each local is then used exactly once when constructing PipelineReport — the indirection adds no clarity and reads as AI padding
Pattern: x = extras.x if extras is not None else None repeated six times
findings.suggestion: Use extras or ReportExtras() pattern then reference fields directly in the PipelineReport constructor call.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py is a re-export facade that adds no policy — callers import through it but logic lives in four split modules
evidence: logging_utils imports from logging_helpers, logging_progress, logging_formatters, and logging_constants, then re-exports everything in __all__
The split modules are not independently documented as stable boundaries
Callers traversing an extra indirection hop with zero benefit; the split was done for file size, not ownership
findings.suggestion: Either collapse the internal split modules back into logging_utils or promote them to explicit stable public packages and let callers import directly.

findings.summary: ReportExtras is a single-use parameter bag that duplicates PipelineReport's optional fields
evidence: ReportExtras has six fields that mirror the optional fields of PipelineReport exactly
Its sole purpose is to bundle the six kwargs of generate_pipeline_report — never stored, mutated, or reused
Docstring describes it as grouping six optional fields — parameter-bag overengineering
findings.suggestion: Remove ReportExtras and accept the optional fields directly as keyword arguments on generate_pipeline_report.

findings.summary: logging_helpers.py uses a function-level import to dodge a circular dependency — no other infrastructure module does this
evidence: logging_helpers.py: 'from infrastructure.core.exceptions import TemplateError' inside the function body, unlike all sibling modules which import at top level
logging_utils.py __all__ re-exports EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING — symbols that conceptually belong in their own modules
findings.suggestion: Move the TemplateError import in format_error_with_suggestions to module level or consolidate logging_helpers into logging_utils if it only exists to hold two helper functions.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py uses try/except ImportError to set names to None — a pattern absent from sibling __init__.py files
evidence: reporting/__init__.py: generate_all_dashboards, generate_matplotlib_dashboard, generate_plotly_dashboard assigned to None on ImportError
validation/__init__.py: all imports are unconditional, no None-fallback pattern
Mixing callable and None in __all__ is a type-safety outlier
findings.suggestion: Either move optional dashboard symbols out of __all__ and document them as optional, or raise a clear ImportError at call time rather than silently setting names to None.

findings.summary: PerformanceMonitor.stop() raises BuildError on misuse but StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() returns empty dict for same precondition
evidence: performance.py: 'raise BuildError("Monitor not started")' in PerformanceMonitor.stop()
performance.py: 'if self.start_time is None: return {}' in StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() — same precondition, opposite strategy
performance_context uses bare finally block that calls stop() unconditionally — if stop() raises during active exception, it replaces the original
findings.suggestion: Align the two tracker classes: either both raise on unstarted state, or both return a sentinel. In performance_context, guard stop() so it does not run when body raised.

findings.summary: performance.py houses two unrelated sub-domains in one module despite explicit acknowledgment that they should be split
evidence: Module docstring explicitly names two distinct sub-domains and anticipates a split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py
PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker and CodeProfiler/ProfilingMetrics have no shared state and zero interdependency
findings.suggestion: Resolve the circular import root cause and split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py as the module itself recommends.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report unpacks ReportExtras field-by-field into local variables, then re-passes them — no-op abstraction
evidence: Six consecutive assignments extract every field of extras into local variables only to pass them directly to PipelineReport
The log-file side-effect (lines 87-94) modifies output_statistics in place, mixing data mutation with report construction inside a factory function
findings.suggestion: Either pass extras fields directly to PipelineReport or let ReportExtras carry its own to_pipeline_report() conversion so the factory stays pure.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py re-exports 30+ items from five split submodules making it a wide aggregation facade with no logic
evidence: __all__ lists 30 symbols spanning formatters, progress utilities, ETA functions, spinner classes, constants
The module imports from logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_constants, logging_progress — pure re-export hub with no logic beyond get_logger/setup_logger
findings.suggestion: Shrink __all__ to the core logging API. Callers that need progress utilities or formatters should import from their respective submodules directly.

findings.summary: Pipeline executor abort-on-failure behavior has no cross-stage integration test
evidence: No test runs execute_full_pipeline or execute_core_pipeline with a mid-pipeline failure and verifies subsequent stages are not invoked
_execute_pipeline breaks on first failure — this abort-on-failure path is the core correctness guarantee with no integration-level test
findings.suggestion: Add a test with one script that exits 1 and verify that execute_core_pipeline returns only results up to and including the failing stage.

findings.summary: CodeProfiler and monitor_performance decorator in performance.py have no tests — only the psutil sub-domain is covered
evidence: test_performance.py imports only psutil-based classes — CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, get_performance_monitor, monitor_performance entirely absent
CodeProfiler.monitor() contains tracemalloc coordination logic that is non-trivial and completely untested
findings.suggestion: Add tests for CodeProfiler.monitor(), benchmark_function(), and the monitor_performance decorator covering metrics appended to history and memory fields populated.
